matches.snippet: ...the sameold gamebold adomic structure of our Finnius the old One, as highly charged with electrons...
matches.reasoning: Direct variant of Finn/Finnegan. Finnius is the Latinate form of the hero's name, representing the ancient/primordial figure.

matches.snippet: Fennsense, finnsonse, awom! Tuck upp those wide shorts.
matches.reasoning: Playful garbling mixing Fenian/Fenn + sense, then finn + sonse (obsolete form of prosperity). Evokes both Finn and the Fianna (Fenians).

matches.snippet: ...like funn make called Foon MacCrawl brothers, mystery man of the pork martyrs?
matches.reasoning: Garbled variant of MacCool/Finn MacCool. 'Foon' phonetically echoes Finn, 'MacCrawl' is deformed MacCool. The 'brothers' reference suggests the Fianna warrior band.

matches.matched_text: Fing! One must simply laugh. Fing him aging!
matches.snippet: ...Music, me ouldstrow, please! We'll have a brand rehearsal. Fing! One must simply laugh. Fing him aging!
matches.reasoning: Direct shortening of Finn. 'Fing him aging' contains the pun 'Finn again', invoking the cyclical resurrection/aging motif central to the wake.

matches.snippet: ...We are all at home in old Fintona, thank Danis, for ourselfsake...
matches.reasoning: Fintona is a Dublin place name containing Fin-. Also echoes both Finn and Finnegan, situating the hero in the landscape.

matches.snippet: ...to Adam, our former first Finnlatter and our grocerest churcher...
matches.reasoning: Compound epithet: 'Finnlatter' combines Finn + later/latter, suggesting Finn as the foundational/ancient figure ('Adam, our former first'), an avatar of primal man.

matches.snippet: ...If all theMacCrawls would only handle virgils like Armsworks, Limited!
matches.reasoning: MacCrawl = garbled MacCool. Used as a plural family name, echoing the extended clan/warrior band of Finn and the Fianna.

matches.snippet: ...In the wake of the blackshape, Nattenden Sone; whenat, hindled firth and bundled furth, the week of wakes is out and over...
matches.reasoning: The 'wake' motif is central: the corpse-watching ritual that frames Finnegan's resurrection. 'Week of wakes' puns on Finnegans Wake itself.
Phoenix (Phoenix Park, associated with HCE's recumbent body) waking/rising. Evokes resurrection and cyclical renewal tied to the Finn/HCE figure.

matches.matched_text: the rise of the man, of the booths
matches.snippet: Away! Rise up, man of the booths, you have slept so long!
matches.reasoning: The sleeping giant awakening after the fall/death. 'Booths' may echo HCE the publican. Central to Finnegan's wake narrative of fall and resurrection.
